1: 'In the year that King Uzziah died' — 740 BCE, after a 52-year reign (2 Kings 15:2). Uzziah's death marks the end of Judah's prosperous mid-8th-century BCE period and the beginning of the Assyrian threat under Tiglath-Pileser III. Isaiah's vision is dated precisely to this hinge-year. 'I saw the Lord seated on a throne, high and exalted, and the train of his robe filled the temple' — the vision takes place in the Jerusalem temple (or a heavenly temple, or both simultaneously — the earthly temple as a copy of the heavenly, as in Exodus 25:9; Hebrews 8:5). The train (shulav, hem/skirt) of YHWH's robe filling the temple is the overwhelming size and glory of the divine presence making even the vast space too small to contain it.
2: 'Seraphim were standing above him, each with six wings: with two wings they covered their faces, with two they covered their feet, and with two they were flying' — the seraphim (serafim, burning/fiery ones) are unique to this vision in the OT; elsewhere cherubim (keruvim) flank the divine throne (Exodus 25:18-20; Ezek 1; 10). 'Seraphim' shares the root with saraf (to burn) and with the copper/fiery serpents (nechash hanesaraph) of Numbers 21:6-9. The six wings serve three functions: covering the face (before the blinding divine holiness), covering the feet (a euphemism for the genitals — modesty before the holy God), and flying (active service). Revelation 4:8 transfers the sixfold wing and trisagion to the four living creatures around the heavenly throne.
3: 'Holy, holy, holy is the LORD of hosts; the whole earth is full of his glory!' — the Trisagion (three-fold holy), the climax of the vision's auditory content. Triple repetition in Hebrew is the superlative: qadosh qadosh qadosh (holy, holy, holy) = absolutely holy, maximally holy, incomparably holy. The holiness of YHWH (his absolute separateness from all that is impure or finite) is the dominant attribute in Isaiah (the 'Holy One of Israel' epithet appears 26x). 'The whole earth is full of his glory (kavod)' — the paradox: the God too vast to fit in the temple (1 Kings 8:27: 'the highest heavens cannot contain you') simultaneously fills the whole earth with his glory. The Trisagion became the Sanctus of the Christian Mass (from the Latin: Sanctus, Sanctus, Sanctus) and is reflected in Revelation 4:8.
4: 'At the sound of the voice that called, the door-frames shook and the entire house filled with smoke' — the theophanic responses: shaking and smoke. The door-frames shaking (yanu'u ammot hassipim, the pivots/foundations of the thresholds moved) reflects the earthquake-response to divine theophany (Psalms 18:7: 'the earth trembled and quaked; the foundations of the mountains shook'; Sinai: Exodus 19:18). The smoke filling the house is the theophanic cloud of YHWH's presence: the same cloud that filled the tabernacle when YHWH took up residence (Exodus 40:34-35) and the temple at its dedication (1 Kings 8:10-11). Smoke/cloud = divine glory that is perceptible but not directly visible.
5: 'Woe is me! I am ruined! For I am a man of unclean lips, and I live among a people of unclean lips, and my eyes have seen the King, the LORD of hosts!' — Isaiah's response is not awe but existential crisis: the holiness of the divine presence exposes the prophet's impurity. The specific sin is lips (sefatayim, lips/speech) — the prophet's instrument is the mouth, and the mouth is unclean. Having pronounced six woe-oracles on others (Isaiah 5), Isaiah now pronounces the woe on himself: 'Woe is me!' (oy li, woe to me). The title 'the King, the LORD of hosts' (hammelech Yhwh tseva'ot) is the political/military sovereignty claim: Isaiah has seen the true king in the year another king died. The prophetic logic: seeing YHWH = facing death (Genesis 16:13; Exodus 33:20; Judges 6:22-23; Isaiah 13:22).
6: 'Then one of the seraphim flew to me, holding in his hand a burning coal that he had lifted from the altar with a pair of tongs' — the burning coal (ritzpah, a live coal/glowing stone) is taken from the altar (mizbeach) with tongs (melqachayim). The altar in the vision is most naturally the incense altar (the altar of burnt offering was in the outer court, not before YHWH's throne; the incense altar was in the Holy of Holies). The coal's holiness: it comes from the altar of divine presence, the place of sacrifice and atonement. The seraph's use of tongs rather than bare hands signals the coal's dangerous holiness — too holy even for the seraphim to touch directly.
7: 'He touched my mouth with it and said: See, this has touched your lips; your guilt is removed and your sin covered over' — the purification of Isaiah's lips by the altar-coal: contact with the holy object transfers holiness and removes impurity (the same logic as the blood of the sacrifice touching the altar in Leviticus 17:6). 'Your guilt is removed' (sar avonecha, your iniquity has departed) and 'your sin covered over' (vachatta'techa techuppar, your sin is atoned/covered). The two terms — iniquity removed and sin atoned — represent both the objective guilt and its judicial covering. The ritual of atonement (kippur) applied directly to the prophet's person enables the prophetic mission that immediately follows.
8: 'Then I heard the voice of the Lord saying: Whom shall I send? And who will go on our behalf? I said: Here I am! Send me' — the divine council deliberation: 'on our behalf' (lanu, for us) echoes the divine council plurals of Genesis 1:26 ('Let us make mankind') and Genesis 11:7 ('Come, let us go down'). Isaiah overhears the divine council's need for a messenger and volunteers: hineni shelachain (here I am, send me). The hineni (here am I) is the standard response of patriarchal availability (Abraham in Genesis 22:1; Moses in Exodus 3:4; Samuel in 1 Samuel 3:4). Isaiah does not wait to be asked — he offers himself before he knows the content of the mission. The hardening commission (Isaiah 6:9-10) reveals that he volunteered for a difficult task without advance knowledge.
9: 'Go and tell this people: You will keep on hearing, but never understand; you will keep on seeing, but never perceive' — the hardening commission: the paradoxical mission to proclaim a message that will not be understood. The hearing-not-understanding and seeing-not-perceiving echoes the wilderness generation (Deuteronomy 29:4: 'the LORD has not given you a mind that understands or eyes that see or ears that hear'). This verse is cited in all four Gospels as the explanation for why many of Jesus's contemporaries rejected the gospel (Matthew 13:14-15 at the parable teaching; Mark 4:12; Luke 8:10; John 12:40) and twice in Acts (Acts 28:26-27 as Paul's final word to Roman Jews). Paul cites it in Romans 11:8 ('God gave them a spirit of stupor') for Israel's partial hardening until the fullness of the Gentiles.
10: 'Deaden the heart of this people, make their ears hard of hearing, and shut their eyes — otherwise they will see with their eyes and hear with their ears and understand with their hearts, and they will turn back and be healed' — the full hardening commission with its stated purpose: to prevent turning-back-and-healing (the repentance-and-restoration that was offered in Isaiah 1:18-20). The 'otherwise...they will turn back' clause reveals the paradox: YHWH is withdrawing the opportunity for repentance by making the prophetic message unintelligible. Theologically, this follows on the rejection of the offer in Isaiah 1: those who persistently reject the covenant word eventually lose the capacity to receive it. John 12:39-40 interprets this as divine action: 'For this reason they could not believe.'
11: 'Then I said: How long, O Lord? He answered: Until cities are devastated and left without a single resident, until houses stand empty of people, and the land is laid waste completely' — the 'How long' (ad-matay, until when?) question is the lament question of the psalms (Psalms 13:1; Isaiah 79:5; Isaiah 89:46). The answer is devastating: the hardening continues until complete desolation. 'Cities devastated' (charu ha'arim) and 'houses without people' (battim me'ein adam) and 'land laid utterly waste' (ha'adamah tishav shamamah) — the three spheres of inhabited life (city, house, land) all emptied. The answer is not 'until they repent' but 'until devastation is complete' — the judgment will run its full course.
12: 'And the LORD has sent people far into exile, and the desolate places are widespread across the land' — the exile (verichaq YHWH et-ha'adam, the LORD has sent humanity far away) is the ultimate covenant curse of Deuteronomy 28:63-64 ('the LORD will uproot you from the land... and scatter you among all nations'). The geographical reach ('far away') mirrors the distant nation summoned in Isaiah 5:26. The 'desolate places widespread' (veravah ha'azuvah, the abandonment is great) — the landscape of the Assyrian aftermath is one of abandoned and depopulated territory, the physical mark of judgment. The exile Isaiah predicts was first fulfilled by the Assyrian deportation of the north (722 BCE) and then by the Babylonian exile (587-586 BCE).
13: 'And even if a tenth should survive, it too will be burned — like a terebinth or an oak that still has its stump after the tree has been cut down. Yet the holy seed will be the stump' — the stump theology: even the remnant (a tenth) will be burned, like a tree cut down. But the stump remains. The terebinth/oak comparison works on two levels: in Israelite ecology, a cut-down oak or terebinth retains its root system and sends up new shoots from the stump; the stump is the guarantee of future growth. 'The holy seed (zera qodesh) will be the stump (matzevet)' — the holy seed is the remnant from which restoration comes. Isaiah 11:1 develops this: 'A shoot will come up from the stump of Jesse; from his roots a Branch will bear fruit.' The stump theology is the basis for all subsequent restoration prophecy.
